section.title: Holiness in Conduct
section.sectionRationale: This is the heart of the Holiness Code, moving from the sexual ethics of chapter 18 to a comprehensive vision of practical holiness touching worship, family, generosity, honesty, and love of neighbor, all grounded in the command to be holy because the LORD their God is holy.
section.thesis: God calls Israel to imitate His holiness in the whole fabric of ordinary life, worship, family, business, justice, and neighbor love, summarized in the command to love your neighbor as yourself, which Jesus later names as the second great commandment.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the call to holiness rooted in God's own character; laws of worship and family respect; provision for the poor through gleaning; laws of honesty, justice, and love among neighbors; prohibitions against occult and pagan customs; and closing laws of fairness to foreigners, the elderly, and in business.

units.label: Be holy, for I am holy

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the whole congregation of Israel and tell them: Be {{qadosh|holy}} because I, the LORD your God, am holy.
Each of you must respect his mother and father, and you must keep My Sabbaths. I am the LORD your God.
Do not turn to idols or make for yourselves molten gods. I am the LORD your God.
When you sacrifice a peace offering to the LORD, you shall offer it for your acceptance.
It shall be eaten on the day you sacrifice it, or on the next day; but what remains on the third day must be burned up.
If any of it is eaten on the third day, it is tainted and will not be accepted.
Whoever eats it will bear his iniquity, for he has profaned what is holy to the LORD. That person must be cut off from his people.

units.label: Gleaning for the poor and the foreigner

verses.text: When you reap the harvest of your land, you are not to reap to the very {{peah|edges}} of your field or gather the gleanings of your harvest.
You must not strip your vineyard bare or gather its fallen grapes. Leave them for the poor and the foreigner. I am the LORD your God.

units.label: Honesty, justice, and love of neighbor
units.note: the great commandment, in seed form

media.alt: Two figures facing each other in a field
media.caption: Love your neighbor as yourself, the command Jesus names as second only to loving God.

verses.text: You must not steal. You must not lie or deceive one another.
You must not swear falsely by My name and so profane the name of your God. I am the LORD.
You must not defraud your neighbor or rob him. You must not withhold until morning the wages due a hired hand.
You must not curse the deaf or place a stumbling block before the blind, but you shall fear your God. I am the LORD.
You must not pervert justice; you must not show partiality to the poor or favoritism to the rich; you are to judge your neighbor fairly.
You must not go about spreading slander among your people. You must not endanger the life of your neighbor. I am the LORD.
You must not harbor hatred against your brother in your heart. Directly rebuke your neighbor, so that you will not incur guilt on account of him.
Do not seek revenge or bear a grudge against any of your people, but {{ahav|love}} your neighbor as yourself. I am the LORD.

You are to keep My statutes. You shall not crossbreed two different kinds of livestock; you shall not sow your fields with two kinds of seed; and you shall not wear clothing made of two kinds of material.

If a man lies carnally with a slave girl promised to another man but who has not been redeemed or given her freedom, there must be due punishment. But they are not to be put to death, because she had not been freed.
The man, however, must bring a ram to the entrance to the Tent of Meeting as his guilt offering to the LORD.
The priest shall make atonement on his behalf before the LORD with the ram of the guilt offering for the sin he has committed, and he will be forgiven the sin he has committed.
When you enter the land and plant any kind of tree for food, you shall regard the fruit as forbidden. For three years it will be forbidden to you and must not be eaten.
In the fourth year all its fruit must be consecrated as a praise offering to the LORD.
But in the fifth year you may eat its fruit; thus your harvest will be increased. I am the LORD your God.

units.label: No occult practices, no pagan customs

verses.text: You must not eat anything with blood still in it. You must not practice divination or sorcery.
You must not cut off the hair at the sides of your head or clip off the edges of your beard.
You must not make any cuts in your bodies for the dead or put tattoo marks on yourselves. I am the LORD.
You must not defile your daughter by making her a prostitute, or the land will be prostituted and filled with depravity.
You must keep My Sabbaths and have reverence for My sanctuary. I am the LORD.
You must not turn to mediums or spiritists; do not seek them out, or you will be defiled by them. I am the LORD your God.

units.label: Fairness to the elderly, the foreigner, and in business

verses.text: You are to rise in the presence of the elderly, honor the aged, and fear your God. I am the LORD.
When a foreigner resides with you in your land, you must not oppress him.
You must treat the {{ger|foreigner}} living among you as native-born and love him as yourself, for you were foreigners in the land of Egypt. I am the LORD your God.
You must not use dishonest measures of length, weight, or volume.
You shall maintain honest scales and weights, an honest ephah, and an honest hin. I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt.
You must keep all My statutes and all My ordinances and follow them. I am the LORD.”

qadosh.gloss: Holy, set apart. The chapter's opening command grounds every following law in God's own character: Israel is to be holy not by achieving abstract purity but by conforming its ordinary conduct to the character of the LORD.

peah.gloss: Edge or corner, here of a harvested field. Later Jewish law developed detailed rules for how much of a field's edge to leave unharvested; the principle itself builds generosity toward the poor directly into the structure of ordinary farming.

ahav.gloss: To love. Verse 18's command to love your neighbor as yourself summarizes the whole preceding list of ethical commands and is later named by Jesus as the second great commandment, on which hangs all the Law and the Prophets, Matthew 22:39-40.

ger.gloss: Foreigner, sojourner, resident alien. Verse 34 extends the command to love one's neighbor explicitly to this outsider, grounding it in Israel's own history: you were foreigners in the land of Egypt.

basic.context: This chapter gives a sweeping catalog of practical holiness, touching worship, family respect, generosity to the poor, honesty, justice, fair business, and love of neighbor, all repeatedly anchored by the refrain, I am the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter mixes enduring moral commands with old covenant ceremonial and civil markers specific to Israel. Love of neighbor, honesty, and care for the poor and vulnerable are reaffirmed throughout the New Testament as binding on all people; certain boundary markers, like mixed fabric or particular hairstyles, belonged to Israel's distinct identity and are not repeated as commands for the church.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with qadosh, holy, grounding every command in God's own character. Its ethical center is ahav, love, in verse 18's command to love your neighbor as yourself, which verse 34 extends explicitly to the ger, the resident foreigner, on the grounds that Israel itself had once been foreigners in Egypt.
basic.typology: The gleaning law is fulfilled literally when Ruth the Moabite gleans in Boaz's field under this very statute, becoming an ancestress of David and ultimately of Christ. The command to love your neighbor as yourself is named by Jesus as the second great commandment and is perfectly embodied in His own self-giving love.
basic.christ: Jesus cites Leviticus 19:18 directly as the second great commandment, on which hang all the Law and the Prophets, Matthew 22:37-40, and He is the one who fulfills it perfectly, loving His neighbor, even His enemies, to the point of laying down His own life, John 15:13.
basic.god: God's holiness here is not abstract ritual purity but concrete ethical practice woven through worship, family, business, and justice. He identifies with the poor, the deaf, the blind, and the foreigner, and grounds nearly every command in His own character: I am the LORD.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 22:37-40 names Leviticus 19:18 the second great commandment. 1 Peter 1:15-16 applies Be holy, for I am holy directly to the church. Romans 13:8-10 says love fulfills the whole law. Ruth 2:2-3 shows the gleaning law in action, providentially placing Ruth in the line leading to Christ.
basic.oneStory: God's people are called, in the smallest details of daily life, to reflect His own holy character; that call reaches its fulfillment in Christ, who is Himself perfectly holy and perfectly loving, and who unites believers to Himself so that, by His Spirit, they can at last begin to live what this chapter commands.

context.literary: This chapter is the ethical heart of the Holiness Code, following chapter 18's sexual ethics and chapter 17's centralized sacrifice, and gathering together commands about worship, family, economics, and justice under a single refrain, I am the LORD, repeated fifteen times.
context.historical: For a people newly formed as a nation and about to enter a land whose customs they were told not to imitate, this chapter supplied a working, practical vision of what daily holiness looked like across every sphere of communal life.
context.canonical: Verse 18's command to love your neighbor as yourself becomes, in Jesus' own teaching, the summary of the law's horizontal demands, quoted directly by Him and repeatedly by Paul and James as the fulfillment of the whole law.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To spell out, in wide ranging concrete cases, what practical holiness looks like across worship, family, economic life, and justice, all grounded in and modeled after God's own character.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter mixes enduring moral commands, love of neighbor, honesty, justice, care for the poor and vulnerable, with old covenant ceremonial and civil markers specific to Israel, such as mixed fabric, mixed seed, and particular grooming customs. Christians rightly treat the moral commands as abiding and reaffirmed in the New Testament, while recognizing the ceremonial boundary markers as belonging to Israel's distinct old covenant identity.

debates.question: What is the point of the seemingly arbitrary prohibitions on mixed breeding, mixed seed, and mixed fabric in verse 19?

views.label: Symbolic boundary markers of created order
views.case: Many see these as living object lessons reinforcing the created order's distinct categories and Israel's own distinctiveness among the nations, similar to other ceremonial boundary markers in the Torah.

views.label: A specific guard against pagan cultic practice
views.case: Others note that certain ancient Near Eastern fertility cults employed mixed breeding, planting, and weaving in ritual contexts, so the law may be a targeted ban on syncretistic superstition rather than a timeless principle about textiles or agriculture.

debates.bearing: Both views treat this as a ceremonial boundary marker specific to Israel's identity and setting, not a moral command binding on Christians; unlike love of neighbor, the New Testament never reaffirms mixed fabric or mixed seed prohibitions as continuing obligations.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's foundation is qadosh, holy, in verse 2, tying every following command to God's own character rather than mere custom. Its ethical high point is ahav, love, in verse 18's command to love your neighbor as yourself, which verse 34 deliberately extends to the ger, the resident foreigner, grounding that extension in Israel's own history as foreigners in Egypt.

typology.type: Gleaning left for the poor and the foreigner
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled literally when Ruth the Moabite gleans in Boaz's field under this very law, becoming, through that provision, an ancestress of David and ultimately of Christ, Ruth 2, Matthew 1:5.
Named by Jesus as the second great commandment and perfectly embodied in His own self-giving love, which lays down its life for others, John 15:13, Romans 13:9-10.

study.christ: Jesus quotes Leviticus 19:18 directly, naming it the second great commandment, on which, with love of God, hang all the Law and the Prophets, Matthew 22:37-40. Where Israel was repeatedly called and repeatedly failed to love its neighbor as itself, Christ alone fulfilled this command perfectly, loving His neighbors and even His enemies to the point of laying down His own life, and by His Spirit now enables His people to begin loving as He loved.
study.god: God's holiness in this chapter is not confined to the sanctuary but extends into ordinary farming, business, family life, and courts of justice; He identifies Himself with the poor, the deaf, the blind, the elderly, and the foreigner, and grounds nearly every command in His own unchanging character, I am the LORD, showing that ethics and worship are never finally separate for Him.

crossReferences.note: Jesus names Leviticus 19:18 the second great commandment, on which hang all the Law and the Prophets.
Be holy, for I am holy is quoted directly and applied to the church's conduct.
Love of neighbor is said to fulfill the whole law.
Ruth gleans in Boaz's field under the very law given in verses 9-10, providentially placing her in the ancestry of Christ.

study.oneStory: God's people are called, in the smallest details of daily life, to reflect His own holy and loving character. That call, repeated and largely unmet through Israel's history, reaches its fulfillment in Christ, who is Himself perfectly holy and perfectly loving, and who unites His people to Himself so that, by His Spirit, they can at last begin to live what this chapter commands.

quiz.q: What is the foundational command given in verse 2, on which the rest of the chapter builds?

options.text: Be holy, because I, the LORD your God, am holy.
options.note: Verse 2 states this as the ground of everything that follows.

options.text: Be strong, because your enemies are near.
options.note: The chapter's ground is God's own holy character, not military circumstance.

options.text: Be wealthy, because the land is fertile.
options.note: The chapter's foundation is holiness modeled on God's character, not material prosperity.

options.text: No foundational command is given.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly gives this foundational command.

quiz.q: What were Israelite farmers commanded to leave for the poor and the foreigner?

options.text: The edges of their fields and the gleanings of their harvest.
options.note: Verses 9-10 specify exactly this provision.

options.text: A tenth of their total annual income in coin.
options.note: This describes a tithe, not the specific gleaning provision named here.

options.text: Nothing; the poor were expected to provide entirely for themselves.
options.note: Verses 9-10 explicitly require leaving gleanings for the poor and foreigner.

options.text: The text gives no instruction about the poor.
options.note: Verses 9-10 explicitly address provision for the poor and foreigner.

quiz.q: According to verse 18, what command culminates the chapter's list of ethical instructions toward others?

options.text: Love your neighbor as yourself.
options.note: Verse 18 gives this command, later cited by Jesus as the second great commandment.

options.text: Avoid your neighbor whenever possible.
options.note: The command given is the opposite: active love of neighbor, not avoidance.

options.text: Repay evil for evil against your neighbor.
options.note: Verse 18 explicitly forbids revenge and grudges, commanding love instead.

options.text: No command regarding neighbors appears in this chapter.
options.note: Verse 18 gives this command directly.

quiz.q: How were Israelites commanded to treat the foreigner living among them, according to verses 33-34?

options.text: As native-born, loving him as themselves, remembering they too were foreigners in Egypt.
options.note: Verses 33-34 state this directly, grounding it in Israel's own history.

options.text: With suspicion, since foreigners could not be trusted.
options.note: The text commands the opposite: not to oppress the foreigner, but to love him as oneself.

options.text: By excluding them entirely from the land.
options.note: Verses 33-34 explicitly command inclusion and fair treatment, not exclusion.

options.text: No instruction regarding foreigners appears here.
options.note: Verses 33-34 explicitly address this.

quiz.q: Which verse from this chapter does Jesus quote as the second greatest commandment?

options.text: Leviticus 19:18, love your neighbor as yourself.
options.note: Matthew 22:39 quotes this verse directly.

options.text: Leviticus 19:2, be holy as I am holy.
options.note: This verse is quoted in 1 Peter 1:16, but it is verse 18 Jesus names as the second great commandment.

options.text: Leviticus 19:9, laws about gleaning.
options.note: This is not the verse Jesus cites as the second great commandment.

options.text: No verse from this chapter is ever quoted in the New Testament.
options.note: Verse 18 is quoted directly by Jesus in Matthew 22:39.

questions: God grounds nearly every command in this chapter with, I am the LORD. Where in your daily life, work, money, speech, do you compartmentalize away from God's character rather than letting it shape you there too?

The gleaning law built generosity to the poor directly into the structure of ordinary farming. What would it look like for you to build similar, structural generosity into your own income or schedule, rather than leaving it to occasional impulse?
Verse 18 moves from forbidding revenge and grudges straight to commanding love. Is there someone you are holding a grudge against right now whom this verse calls you to actively love instead?
Jesus names love of neighbor the second great commandment. Who is the neighbor it is hardest for you to love as yourself right now, and what would obedience look like this week?
Israel was told to love the foreigner because they themselves had been foreigners in Egypt. What past experience of your own could God be using to grow your compassion for someone different from you?

translationNotes.note: Place a stumbling block before the blind is likely both a literal command and, by extension, a principle against deliberately exploiting anyone's particular vulnerability or disadvantage.

Neighbor translates a Hebrew word, rea, whose scope this very chapter widens: verse 34 explicitly extends the same command, and the same word love, to the resident foreigner, anticipating the broader neighbor love Jesus later teaches in the parable of the Good Samaritan.

The prohibition on cutting the hair at the sides of the head and the edges of the beard likely targeted specific pagan mourning or cultic customs of the surrounding nations, rather than establishing a universal, timeless grooming law.
